items.mistake: Picking polynomial degree by best training error
items.fix: Select degree with k-fold cross-validation or a held-out validation set; prefer the simplest degree within one standard error of the best CV score.
items.why: Training error always improves as degree rises, even when the model is memorizing noise.
items.example: Degree 8 may hit R^2 = 0.99 on 20 training points but fail on validation; degree 3 with R^2 = 0.92 generalizes better.

items.fix: Apply StandardScaler (or similar) to inputs before `PolynomialFeatures`, especially when |x| can be large.
items.why: Columns like x and x^5 live on vastly different scales, making the normal equations ill-conditioned and coefficients unstable.
items.example: With x in [1000, 2000], x^5 is ~ 10^16 while x is ~ 10^3—ridge or OLS weights become numerically fragile without scaling.

items.mistake: Calling it non-linear regression in the optimization sense
items.fix: Describe it as linear regression on polynomial features; use non-linear optimizers only when you change the functional form (e.g. y = a*exp(b*x)).
items.why: Mislabeling leads teams to skip closed-form solvers, omit regularization, or misinterpret coefficient linearity.
items.example: `LinearRegression` after `PolynomialFeatures(degree=3)` is correct; `curve_fit` on y = a*x^b is a different, genuinely non-linear problem.
